Supervised Machine Learning with Decision Trees and Ensemble Methods

Ted Ellsworth

3/4/2019

A Brief Introduction to Supervised Machine Learning

  • Introduction to Statistical Learning
    • Theory
    • Underlying Mechanics
  • Classification and Regression Trees (CART)
  • Ensemble Methods: Bootstrap Aggregating (Bagging), Random Forest

Why Study Machine Learning for Political Science?

  • Powerful tool for prediction problems: violence, electoral outcomes, classification, conflict, etc.

  • Computationally efficient means for obtaining the true functional form of your model

  • Useful for causal inference (Imai and Strauss)
    • Identify heterogenous treatment effects
    • Matching
    • Imputing missing data

  • This is not new!

Foundations of Statistical Learning I

  • Supervised Learning: Predict or estimate an output \(\hat{Y}\) based on a set of inputs \(\hat{f}(x)\).

  • Unsupervised Learning: We observe the inputs \(\hat{f}(x)\), but include no measure of the output. Our objective is to observe the relationships and structure of our data.

  • In both cases we are less interested in the functional form of \(\hat{f}\) so long as it yields accurate predictions. We measure predictive performance as a function of reducible error.

Foundations of Statistical Learning II

  • Parametric Statistics: Based on prior assumptions and theory, estimate the functional form of \(\hat{f}\)

    • Example: \(\hat{f}(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_iX_i +\epsilon\)

  • Nonparametric Statistics: Estimate \(\hat{f}\) without making assumptions about its functional form. Our objective is to get close to the true value of \(f\) without overfitting our model.

  • Accuracy and Interpretability Trade-Off: less flexible models tend to be easier to interpret, but predictions are less accurate. More flexible models may yields more accurate estimates of \(\hat{Y}\), but are difficult to interpret.

Foundations of Statistical Learning III

Accuracy vs. Interpretability

Foundations of Machine Learning IV

  • The Prediction Setting: with machine learning we are less interested in observed values (training data) and more concerned with future events (test data). Machine learning involves (at minumum) a three step process.
      1. Split the model into training and test data
      1. Fit a model to the training data minimize reducible error (MSE, AIC, ROC, etc.)
      1. Apply this model to the test data and extract the error.
  • More mathmatically we say that we are not interested in whether \(\hat{f}(x_i) \approx y_i\) but rather whether \(\hat{f}(x_0) \approx y_0\) where \((x_0, y_0)\) is the previously unseen testing data.

Foundations of Machine Learning V

  • Test Error: the expected test error consists of three quantities
    • The variance of \(\hat{f}(x_0)\)
    • The squared bias of \(\hat{f}(x_0)\)
    • The variance of the error term \(\epsilon\)

  • Variance: how different would \(\hat{f}\) be if we had used a different training set.

  • Bias: the amount of error introduced by estimating a real-life problem.

  • Bias Variance Trade-Off: as model flexibility increases, bias will decrease and variance will increase.

Foundations of Machine Learning VI

  • Accuracy: = \(\frac{Y + Y_f}{Y + Y_f + N + N_f}\)
  • Precision: = \(\frac{Y}{Y + Y_f}\)
  • Recall: = \(\frac{Y}{Y+ N_f}\)
  • Cohens Kappa: \(-1 \leq \kappa \leq 1\)

Foundations of Machine Learning VII

  • One common solution to the problem of variance is cross-validation

  • Cross-validation: partitions a set of \(n\) observations into non-overlapping groups or folds.

  • Each fold acts as a validation set with the final model estimate being an averaged sum between partitions.

  • Limits overfitting and reduces variance in testing procedure.

Foundations of Machine Learning VIII

Cross-Validation: ESL

Pros and Cons of Tree-Based Learning

  • When the DGP is complex tree-based methods are more effective at detecting nonlinearities, discontinuities, and interactions.

  • Mimic the process of human decision making.

  • Decomposition methods allow interpretation which is much less dependent on (weak) analytical assumptions.

  • Not as powerful as other ML methods (Hence we use Bagging and RF)

  • Tree based models are “greedy” in that they bias categorical variables which are easier to split (there are pros and cons to this).

Decision Trees for Political Science Research

  • Tree based methods partition the data space into different regions and fit a simple model to each one.

    • The goal is to find partitions \(R_1,...R_J\) that minimize the reducible error.
    • For recursive binary splitting, we select the predictor \(X_j\) and the cutpoint \(s\)

  • The predictive result of this model can be derived as: \[\hat{f}(X) = \sum_{m=1}^s c_mI{(X_i)\in R_m}\]

Decision Trees

CART and Partitions: ESL

Tree Growing

  • Define regions for all \(X_i\) and cutpoints \(c\)

  • Find split \(s\) which maximizes the reduction in node impurity

\[ \Delta I (s,\tau) = I(\tau)- p(\tau_l)I(\tau_l)-p(\tau_R)I(\tau_R)\] - Where I is a function of \(\hat{p}_k\) the proportion of observations from class k in node \(\tau\).

Example 1: Ethnic Conflict and Civil War

Civil War Onset w/CART

Tree Pruning

  • We want to start with a large tree, but this is not advisable for prediction purposes.

  • To prune the tree, the standard practice is to use cost-complexity pruning (CP).

  • We can define the CP as: \[C_\alpha(\tau) = \sum^{\tau}_{m=1}N_m Qm(\tau)+ \alpha|\tau|\]

  • small values of \(\alpha\) result in smaller trees and large \(\alpha\) large trees.

Example 2: Large Tree Predicting Asylum Grants

Example 2: Cost Complexity and Model Error

Example 2: Pruned Decision Tree (Min)

Example 2: Pruned Decision Tree (1SE)

Bootstrap Aggregating (Bagging)

  • Bagging simulates the process of aggregating the results from multiple training datasets.
  • Three step process:
  1. Draw B bootstrap samples from the training data.
  2. Build a prediction model \(\hat{f}^*bx\) for each sample using base learner
  3. Compute the combined prediction \(\hat{f}_bag(x)\) over all samples

  • Regression: \(\frac{1}{B}\sum^{B}_{b=1}\hat{f}^{*b}(x)\)

  • Classification: \(arg max_k\hat{f}_bag(x)\)

The Random Forest

  • Special case of bagging where we use of subset of features per split

  • With bagging we are interested in averaging over B trees to decrease variance

  • Random forests go one step further by also decorrelating trees.

  • To decorrelate estimates we randomly sample on rows and columns. Meaning we sample \(B\) trees from \(M\) predictor subsets.

Mechanics of a Random Forest

  • A random forest \[{\tau_b}^{B_1}\] consists of a set of b = 1,2,…,B trees for prediction.

  • For regression problems we average predictions over all trees: \[\hat{f}^B_{rf}(x)= \frac{1}{B}\sum^{B}_{b=1} \tau_b(x)\]

  • For classification we are interested in the majority vote among trees:

\[\hat{C}^{B}_{rf}(x) = majorityvote{\hat{C_b(x)}}^{B}_{1}\]

Mechanics of a Random Forest II

Random Forest Voting Cohort

What do we want to learn from our models?

  1. Are the features that I have selected important? Is that importance conditional?
  2. What is the shape of the relationship between features and outcome?
  3. How reliable is my model’s interpretation of the data?

Measuring Model Accuracy

  • A ROC curve is constructed by plotting the true positive rate (TPR) against the false positive rate (FPR).

  • The ROC curve shows the trade-off between sensitivity (or TPR) and specificity (1 – FPR).

  • Classifiers that give curves closer to the top-left corner indicate a better performance.

  • Useful when dependent feature is imbalanced or working with rare events.

  • ROC curves, out of sample test error, concusion tables, etc. give us similar information.

Partial Dependency

  • Partial dependency plots show the marginal effect of one more more features on the predicted outcome (Friedman 2001)

  • We can define dependency as:

\[\hat{f}_{xs}(X_x) = E_{x_c}[\hat{f}(x_s, x_c)] = \int \hat{f}(x_s, x_c)d\mathbb{P}(x_C)\] - \(S\) represents the marginal effect on the outcome and \(x^{i}_{c}\) are the actual feature values.

  • PDPs have a causal intepretation. We intervene on a feature and observe a change in prediction.

  • Note that this can obscure dependencies and heterogenous effects.

Partial Dependency Plots

Partial Dependency Plot: Civil War States

Individual Conditional Expectations and Local Inference

  • ICE is a measure of the predictive performance of a feature for each local observation.

  • Formally for each observation we estimate \({(x^{i}_{S}}, x^{i}_{c})^N\) and derive the curve \(\hat{f}^{(i)}_{S}\).

  • It is a good idea to center each observation at the beginning of the observed feature space. This makes it very easy to interpret.

Ethics and Machine Learning

  • The data we collect and use are not devoid of prejudices and misrepresentations
    • ‘Big-data’ tends to increase the scale of the problem.
    • If the training data include biases, the predicted outcomes will reflect them.

  • The view that ML is unbiased tends to obfuscate public practice: digital redlining, micro-targeting, price discrimination.

  • Sensitive political questions and predictions should be pursued with caution.
    • Can inform deterministic policy reasoning.
    • The same tools that enable us to predict outcomes in political space can inform how leaders respond to political challenges for better or for worse.
